sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Spinal motion restriction is used when there is a meaningful concern for unstable spinal injury. The goal is to minimise unnecessary movement during extrication and transport without creating avoidable delay, pain, pressure injury, or airway compromise.

sections.title: Indications
sections.content:
- Use when spinal assessment suggests risk of unstable spinal injury and the patient cannot be clinically cleared
- Match the degree of restriction to the patient, mechanism, symptoms, and operational setting
- Do not let packaging delay airway intervention, haemorrhage control, rapid extrication, or time-critical transport

sections.title: Full Immobilisation Package
sections.content:
1. Choose the simplest method that limits unnecessary spinal movement while preserving access to airway, breathing, and haemorrhage control

2. Maintain manual support during key movements where needed, and use a collar only if it helps and is tolerated

3. Use low-movement extrication techniques and encourage controlled self-extrication when appropriate and safer

4. Package the patient on a vacuum mattress or other preferred transport surface where available; minimise time on hard boards used for extrication

5. Secure the patient sufficiently to prevent excess movement during transfer and transport

6. Reassess pain, neurological status, airway, breathing, and comfort after every major move

sections.title: Advanced Practice Points
sections.content:
- Modify the package when airway management, ventilation, entrapment, obesity, pregnancy, agitation, or other factors make standard positioning unsuitable
- Coordinate moves carefully to reduce uncontrolled spinal movement during advanced procedures or aeromedical transfer
- Escalate early if neurological findings are evolving or the patient's condition is too unstable for prolonged scene packaging

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Vacuum mattress or equivalent low-pressure support is preferred for transport where available
- Hard boards are primarily extrication tools and should not be used longer than necessary
- A perfect package is less important than safe movement, time-critical care, and ongoing reassessment
- Document the assessment result, devices used, patient tolerance, and any modification made for clinical reasons
